speaker: interviewer
text: Number 6.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to mention, [CLEARS THROAT] before we start leaving Germany or Western-- the Americans are [INAUDIBLE] to come to the United States-- I want to mention-- speak about our fellow Jews and my extended family, Hruszwica. Hruszwica was a nice little village, poor. But in childhood, you remember it as a nice place. We had eight Jewish families. There were actually two distinct families. All the children of these two families, you know, married, had families of their own. And I would like to mention each one of them. We'll start with our maternal family. My mother was one of eight or nine. But I-- I-- I always lose count. My grandparents, Yitzhak and Sarah Tesler had these children. There was Chava. Chava was married and had a daughter, Mania, who lives now in Israel. There was Meyier, but he didn't live in our village at the time of the destruction. He lived in a-- he married-- when he was married to this lady, Bella, he lived in another part of the-- part of the state. Then there was Pinchas, my uncle Pedro who died in Brazil about three, four years ago. My uncle Moses, Mojsze. He lived also in Brazil. I don't know if he's still alive or not. But I get a letter once in a while from them, but no mention of their father. Then there was my mother, Rose, who was raised in Hruszwica. There was my aunt, Malka, my aunt Chana, and an aunt Sheyndel. My Aunt Sheyndel was married to a Mr.-- to my uncle-- my uncle by marriage, Shabtai Steinboim. He perished. But his-- his son-- two sons survived. He had Pinchas and Yitzhak. Both live in Israel. And they are also cousins to Mania. So you see, I'm talking about the Teslers. All these are interrelated. They had a little sister, called Datia. She perished. You see, Eva, Chava, the first daughter, she was a widow. And she married-- she remarried. She married Shabtai, because Sheyndel, the first wife of Shabtai, died giving birth to Datia-- Datia. So later on, the two got together, you know, a widower and a widow. You know. So they already in the family. He married Eva. He perished. He and Pinchas were walking from one village to another. And they were caught in a gully. And Pinchas was shot in the leg. But he was younger. He got out of the gully and survived. But Shabtai, his father, was wounded. And they threw him into a well. Bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, there was Malka and Chana, two sisters on my mother's. They survived. They were one-- Malka Uster and her son, and her husband, came into our village, because they couldn't stay in Klevan, because over there, is a city. And right away, the ghettos [INAUDIBLE]. So they stayed in our village. And Chana lived in Rowne, but her husband, Moses [? Melamud-- ?] Moses, was in the Polish army before the war. So when the war broke out, he stayed in the army. Never came back. Either he died on the front lines-- whichever. He never showed up. But they-- she-- they had a son, Rachmiel. And now he's [? Emmuel. ?] He lives in New York. His mother remarried, so he's actually [? Emmuel ?] [? Marder. ?] He went to his-- took his step-father's name. But I don't think he ever knew his father, because he was just a little baby. They came to live in our village. And they survived. But Mr. Uster, Abraham Uster, that came from Klevan to live in the village, he did not know-- did not understand the art of survival. He was told to stay put, not to go out. He used to walk down the street. And strangers, and especially strange Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had none in our society, right? In any society, a strange person-- an outsider moves in, everybody becomes suspicious. Well, he went for work one day, and never came back. Who knows what happened to him? No one knows. So but my grandma lived there too, which-- Grandma Sarah Tesler. When-- we'll come back to that, what happened to grandma. Next door to my Grandma Tesler lived the Adlers, also known as Korns. And they had, you know, Mr. Mrs. Korn-- or Adler. Him and his-- the two of them, they had two little children, a boy-- a little boy and a girl, who's names I do not remember. And they had four other children. There was Joseph-- [YIDDISH], and [? Reubin, ?] and [? Hershel, ?] and Dvora. Out of that family-- how many is that? Nine? Only two survived, Joe and Dvora. Joe used to live in Brooklyn. He just died last year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dvora was hidden out among Czech people. You know, when you're liberated, you just like to see each other. Everybody's going his own way. You know, how did you survive? Well, blah, blah, blah. story, you know? Well, you came from hell. So did I. You're story is not any different than mine. So you actually do not dwell to know all the little fine details. And besides, what's the use? So you left people alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- the Korns had three aunts living there. There was Sarah Yedlin. And she had three sons and a daughter. Sarah Yedlin was a widow. The Yedlin children-- Sarah's children were contemporaries of my mother and her brothers and sisters-- same age. All five of them survived, as a unit. I don't know how, but they survived, all five. Then there was-- you know, I'm going according to geographical layout of where they lived. Then, there were the Kobylanskis, my father and my mother, my brother, and I. This is number two family that survived. We went into hiding, and we came out. This is amazing, by anybody's account.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't forget, our Grandmother Kobylanski lived also in the same village with us. She lived one time-- you know, what they do with old people? You shift them. One-- one-- sometime, they live with one son. Then, they live with another son, or son and daughter, whatever. Shared. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandma Kobylanski lived with Uncle Chaim on the other end of town. And Uncle Chaim married-- I told you earlier-- married a cousin of my mother's, who I found out was-- I thought she was always my aunt, but I didn't realize she was a cousin also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she lived with them. They had two daughters-- a little girl, Chaika, and a little smaller one over there. And Reizel [INAUDIBLE], you know, the wife of Chaim, she had a brother, Jonathan, who also lived there. He survived. Reizel did not survive. Reizel got thrown into a well. But I assume, probably, after torture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaika, Uncle Chaim's daughter, perished in one day. She was in [INAUDIBLE] for a few days. She came to her father. And her father thought he was doing her a favor. He sent her to another village, which was known to be anti-Semitic, hateful, this group. And before she got into that village, she was killed. She was my brother's age. I was always-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Down the sheet, lived Yechiel Halperin, where the synagogue was. His wife, Reizel-- was a lot of Reizels in the village-- three-- she was related to Mr. Adler. So there was Sarah Yedlin. And this Reizel Halperin. And there was Chava, Eva, [? Heichen. ?] They lived also in the village. The [? Heichens ?] had-- sorry. Yechiel and Reizel and their daughter, Bella perished. Yechiel perished, I think, he was the first victim. Yechiel was for-- from another town, Lutsk. And he was, you know, a bearded man, you know, nice, nice man. He was not a rabbi-- far from. I mean, he was-- he could conduct services.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was like a pastor, a little bit, you know? He never claimed to be a rabbi. But because of a beard, the beard is always associated with a rabbi. And they are troublemakers. He vanished. I assume that they probably came, dragged him out of the house. And maybe who knows where he's buried. Too bad. A very nice, polite gentleman disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: But his wife and daughter, Bella, survived for a little while. [? Heichen ?] which was-- Eva [? Heichen ?] was a sister to Reizel, Sarah, and [NON-ENGLISH] Adler. They had three children. Osher, was his name, the husband's name, and Chava. And they had a daughter, [? Aronia ?] and Rebecca, and Jacob. They like disappeared from the face of the earth. No trace. It's-- it's hard to understand, you know, how things happened like that. No one knows what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember, when-- when that-- when the-- when the mayor of the village came to tell us that the Jews are going to be liquidated? Well, everybody ran his own way. There was no organized anything, no network, nothing. So the older-- the women-- the men, you know, took chances, you know? Like my-- like-- like my father and my mother. They already had a-- some kind of a idea what to do. Besides, he was taught by Mr. Czepko to disappear, how to hide himself, you know? How to survive, the art of survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have my grandma, who's an old lady already. She must have been in her '60s. Maybe she was in her '70s. You don't ask your grandma how old they are when you're eight, nine years old, or 10 years old. Grandma's grandma. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my grandma, and my aunt Chava, and her daughter, Mania, and my little cousin, Datia-- Datia Steinboim-- she was a aunt, and her stepmother, at the same time, they were hidden for a while. And they got chased out. They had no place where-- there was no room at the inn. They came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Grandma Adler came home too, with her daughter-in-law, and the two little kids. Came home. And they stayed there. And Reizel, her parent, and their daughter, Bella, you know, they're not going to live over there by themselves. They came. So the Adlers and the Tesler was neighbors. There were two houses. There was enough room for them to stay together.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandma Kobylanski lived-- you know was [YIDDISH]? Pig sty was, was [YIDDISH] the state was the way you took care of-- well, we were hidden. She lived like a half a block. That was Uncle Chaim's house. So she stayed there with her little granddaughter. Her little granddaughter did not know Yiddish. The rest of us in the village, all that them now, even Chaika, her sister, knew how to speak Yiddish. She was the only one that did not speak Yiddish. When I used to go and visit there, I could not understand. How is it possible? Here's a Jewish little girl who speaks no Yiddish. You know, eh-- it's like comment to an American. You say look at it. All these little kids speak English. Terrific. You forget something there, you know? [INAUDIBLE]. So my Grandma Kobylanski and her little granddaughter-- must have been four-- three or four years old-- they stayed together over there. I think people-- from what I understand, people looked after them. So when the destruction of the Jews was issued, and they came to gather them up, they went to my Grandma Tesler's house, and the Korn's house, Korn-Adler. And they gathered them all up, and they took them out. You know, you saw the picture of the forest. And they took them all over there, and they murdered them. The people that did the murder, the shooting, the killing of them, were not natives. They were people from another village. This way, you can accuse the natives of doing it. You do the dirty work in my village, and I'll do your dirty work in your village. Who's going to know the difference? Strangers came and did it, not us. Tricky, isn't it? Very-- very thought-- figured out how to do, how to carry out this charge. And who did it? The college students, the gymnasiums, the intellectuals. How do I know that? When we were traveling from Stakh's place to Zumer's place, we got caught in daylight. There was a man called Ivan, Ivan Denishchuk, who was, you know, a wiseguy of the village-- not a wise guy. He was kind of a thinking man. Poor as hell can be. Very poor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a witness to that. Because my cousin, Mania, was standing naked at the pit to be killed. And I don't know how it happened. Ivan was standing there, a little-- a little ways, you know, watching the procedures.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whether he went, or whether he signaled her, or what, she ran-- walked away from the pit to him. He took off his shirt and coat and gave it to her. And she survived. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousin Mania never talked about it. Besides, she was older than me. And you don't talk to no little teenagers. She was not old. She must have been, maybe, seven, eight years older. She had owed me no accounting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, who asked? You know, you didn't. You did not have the [INAUDIBLE], you know, the idea that you should ask, how did you survive? Who helped you? Where did you hide out? We didn't talk about it. Besides, when you finally got the smarts that you should do, you were ashamed. You're going to ask somebody, you know, tell me, how did you survive? You know, it's like-- ah, he says we'll talk about it some other time. Now is not the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: People didn't want to talk. They were painful, because they lost their relatives. They were pained-- in pain, because they were ashamed they had to go through all of this indignity, that they had to be in-- in-- whatever, the craziest place. That's where you're supposed to be hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you go and pick somebody's feeling, not pick a brain, pick their feelings? It's a different idea of picking a brain and picking feelings. It's very, very tough, unless the person volunteers. When we were in Florence [INAUDIBLE] Israel in 1973, and then again about 10-- in 1983, all right, we spent some time with Pinchas, at his house. And we talked, you know? And I sort of start asking Pinchas. You know, he's my cousin. So he has no father, and he has no mother, and he has no stepmother either. Those are-- even stepmothers are comforting people. And besides, he was abandoned. All the other relatives moved away, and he was abandoned-- him and his brother, Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother rescued him. You know, we were-- when we were going to Poland from Russia, my mother went and picked him up once, and another time, and another time. But all the other uncles and aunts were gone. So naturally, he was-- maybe had the first time to tell it to somebody about his feelings. [VOICE BREAKS] Well, we had a nice cry that day-- that night. And I'm so glad it was at night. I think it's much easier to take it at night than during the day. But Pinchas was here in the United States. He came from Israel on a tour. And he stayed with us. He made a side trip, you know, left the tour. And he came and stayed with us. I took off from work a whole week. Didn't go to work, and we just gallivanted around. That was nice. I lost a week's wages, but it was a pleasure. We survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: As your family prepared to go to America, what cities were you in just before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we lived in a DP camp. Oh yeah, back to-- to-- we haven't come to America yet. We lived in-- in Ulm, which was a DP camp, a displaced persons camp, which was administered by the Americans to the best of their abilities, which was very generous. Very generous, looking at it now. Maybe we ate a lot of canned Irish potatoes. At that time, they didn't taste so good, but they taste very nice now. And I'm very grateful to all those nice Americans for taking care of us. [CRYING] In Ulm, we have to make document-- we were in the DP camp, naturally, we have to make documents, you know, and see the consulate. And the consulate's in Munich, so we used to travel back and forth. My parents, naturally, took care of those things, my father, especially. Uncle Jack went with him too. Then there was the cousins, because-- the Kromirs cousins, Chaim, Chaika. And Chaika's children came from Russia. And everybody was running around making documents, because [INAUDIBLE]. Americans can't-- they didn't understand that, you know, we came over there with no documents, no papers. And you have to-- for everything, you have to have a document.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the United States-- we lived in Detroit with Burlingame and Dexter. And I used to leave the house, and I didn't have my wallet. I forgot my documents. We had a little card issued to us. I ran right-- I dropped everything, friends, acquaintances. I ran to the house to get my document. You have to have documents, you know? They brainwashed. So then I-- the papers, in Munich, they finally were approved. When we used to go to Munich for, you know, the consulate, we all went, because you never know, they want to see who's there. Who are these people there? But they never called-- you know, they did call us, I guess. Yeah. They did call us. We went to the consulate in Munich. We got the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt [? Blooma ?] and Uncle Max, you have the Frumins here in Detroit, they arranged all this ship-- they paid in advance for the trip. And you know, you have to have money to come. You just can't get on a ship and go. The Frumins were very generous to us-- bring us over here. And we went to Bremerhaven, which was interesting city. You know, it was-- bombed the hell out of it. If you stood in one end of the city, you looked to the other, it was nothing but rubble. I mean, it was a pleasure thing to see, but you look-- you know, you have to understand that's destruction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even if you have pleasure, it's only temporary pleasure, because you still think of people. Maybe these people were nice. Maybe there were decent human beings, and-- but you only see the destruction, the bricks, and the twisted iron beams, and stuff like that. But then you think, what about my grandmas? What about my cousins? You know, you try to justify it, but it's not a nice feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremerhaven, they told that the ship is going to take a month for it to come back, or whatever it's coming from. And finally, the ship came, Actually, it was a troop carrier during the war, because at every floor in the ship had hammocks, lots of hammocks. When we got on the ship, finally, the sleep quarters full, maybe. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we used to go down into the mess room, you know, stand in a line. That's where we discovered hot dogs. Hot dogs, you know? Everybody thought they're really eating a dog food, you know? Then it turns out sausages, you know? So it was nice, the dog. You could have all the bread you wanted. You could have two servings, you know? But you gotta be-- you took two hot dogs and whatever else they had. You filled up. How much could you eat? Beside, they told, don't eat-- don't eat too much, unless you might get seasick. Well, I got seasick anyway. And Chunkys. Do you remember Chunky candies? Oh, I got an allowance-- I don't know how much money. And we used to go to the canteen and buy Chunky candy bars and Coca Cola. Oh, what kind of drink you want? (SHRUGS) You know, you remember, the man said, all the teenagers in America drink Coca Cola. Hm. All right, I'll try it. Sweet stuff. Yuck. Couldn't handle it. So I ate those Chunkys, you know, before we had the storm. Naturally, everything went overboard. [LAUGHS] Somebody spread a rumor on the ship, there's a shortage of bread in America. My father issued a order. Every time you go to the cafeteria, get a couple-- you know, a few extra slices of bread, and bring it down below to the-- to the-- you know, where we-- to our rooms, or to our hall. We'll hide it in bags. Where can you get a bag on the ship? So we used to put it in hammocks, you see? And cover it up with a blanket. When we got to New York, we have to get off the ship. You know, the documents-- the guy says, you're not signing the-- the papers that everything is in order. What do you mean? He says you left the bread. I said, I'm not going to pick up the bread. You-- it's your bread, you take it with you. What should we do? He said, that's up to you. Well, we finally figured out-- we threw it all overboard and [LAUGHING] [INAUDIBLE]. You know? So that's crazy. Uncle Max and Aunt Bertha Frumin were waiting for us on-- on the-- on the-- shore. You could-- my father recognized them, because he knew them from Europe, from before. My sister-- well, she left when she was a young lady. And he his-- her-- his wife-- I mean, his Uncle Max. So we got down there. And he said the train is ready. We're going to leave tonight for Detroit. He took us out to dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, We went some restaurant. In this-- ooh-- horrendous city with these tall buildings, and the streets, and the people running, and the-- this traffic. Horrendous. It's all-- so much electricity all over the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got into a restaurant. And what are you going to eat? What can read English, you know? Who knows? He ordered from the waiter for me-- toast, and sunny side up, and something else, and-- well. You like sunny side up? Who knows. Yeah. My father said, yeah, he'll eat it. Well, the sunny side up came, and there's two floating eyes, you know, these two egg-- the yolks are still jiggling. It's floating in this pool of butter. [THUMP AND SPLASH] This-- this is eggs? Who eats raw eggs? Not me. So I just ate, you know, the whites and the toast.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the train. I don't remember what my brother ate, or my parents, but I was fascinated by those yolks. You know, it's floating in this-- and supposed to eat them. We came to Detroit. I have a picture when we arrived at Michigan Central, which was the big station. It was different. They took us home with them at-- on Burlingame Street. We lived with them for about a month, or maybe more. It was different, let me tell you. Nice beds with mattresses and blankets. And one person to a bed. Wow. When we were in Ulm, you know, the DP camp, we slept on those canvas army bag, you know, canvas, like folding bed, they used to use in the Army. Oh, those are murder. They're only good for one night. After that, you throw them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you go camping sometime-- you know, you have those folding beds, you know, with canvas? You spend more than a night on them, god almighty, a mattress is delicious. Wonderful. But-- So what did you do as a young man, after living in Detroit for a month?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to work down the-- down Dexter Boulevard. Every day, I would go two or three blocks, you know. Like I-- I expanded my territory, like one of those young wolfs. I used to really like stories about wolfs. You know the story It's a very nice, interesting book. You know, you expand your territory.
